items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Two US troops KIA in Jordan — Iran suspends the interim memorandum, the last diplomatic fire door
items.idea: Iranian ballistic missiles and drones killed two US service members and wounded several more at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan. Washington hit back at Iranian missile/radar sites near Hormuz; Tehran formally suspended the interim memorandum that had been the only remaining diplomatic off-ramp. Brent surged toward $88/bbl Friday on falling Hormuz traffic plus reports of extra US aerial refueling tankers moving toward Israel.
items.angle: This is the moment the trade stops being 'geopolitical weather' and starts being a regime-change trade. Watch whether oil, the dollar, and credit spreads start moving together — that's the tell it's systemic, not just an energy shock.
items.tweetA: Two dead US troops turned Trump's Iran red line into a mandate. Interim memorandum suspended — the last diplomatic exit is gone. Watch if oil, the dollar AND credit spreads move together this week; that's the tell it's not just an energy shock anymore. $USO $UUP
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching Brent at $88. The more important number: how many aerial refueling tankers just moved toward Israel. Tankers don't predict targets — they buy Washington the option to sustain a longer campaign it hasn't decided to run yet.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Gulf infrastructure war: desalination, power and a first-ever hit on a US facility in Saudi Arabia
items.idea: Kuwait reported damage to oil, power and desalination infrastructure; Iran reportedly hit a US facility in Saudi Arabia for the first time in months; over 100 communications towers went dark in Hormozgan. The war is now hitting civilian infrastructure across five Gulf states (Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Saudi) rather than just naval/military targets.
items.angle: Desalination is existential in the Gulf, not symbolic — hitting it is the fastest way to drag previously-neutral Gulf states into direct alignment with Washington and to force insurance/shipping desks to reprice regional risk, not just Hormuz transit risk.
items.tweetA: Desalination plants just entered the target set in the Gulf war. In a region with almost zero fresh water, that's not infrastructure damage — that's civilian survival damage. This is the fastest way to drag Qatar/Saudi/Bahrain off the sidelines.
items.tweetB: First strike on a US facility in Saudi Arabia in months, plus Kuwait's power and water hit in the same weekend. The 'contained regional conflict' framing died over 48 hours. Insurance and shipping desks should already be repricing Gulf risk, not just Hormuz transit.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (via Goldman Sachs)
items.title: Kimi K3 charges MORE than every other Chinese model — and still tops the leaderboard
items.idea: Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 (2.8T params, released July 17) ranked #1 in Code Arena and hit near-SOTA on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, per Goldman Sachs (Ronald Keung et al). Its blended API price, ~$2.30/M tokens, is above every other major Chinese model Goldman tracked — a reversal of the DeepSeek-era cost-collapse playbook.
items.angle: If a Chinese lab can charge a premium and still win share, the 'China AI = pure deflationary force on US model margins' trade thesis needs revisiting — at least for coding/agentic workloads, where pricing power is bifurcating from commodity text inference.
items.tweetA: K3 just did something DeepSeek never did: charge MORE than the competition and still top the leaderboard. $2.30/M tokens, priced above every other major Chinese model, and it's #1 on Code Arena. The 'Chinese models = race to zero' thesis just took a real hit.
items.tweetB: Everyone priced China AI as a deflationary force on US model margins. Kimi K3 says: only for commodity text inference. Coding and agentic workloads are bifurcating into a premium tier — exactly where Anthropic and OpenAI make their money too.

items.title: Why the Aeva/SOA fix for CPO lasers is an assembly nightmare, not a laser upgrade
items.idea: An 8-wavelength CPO/NPO laser module built from cheap discrete lasers plus a LiDAR-orphan SOA (e.g. Aeva's 650mW-rated chip) needs 32-40 lenses, 8-16 isolators and 56+ active alignments — vs. Broadcom/Lumentum's monolithic advantage of a shorter cavity length that cuts both cost and mode-hop risk. Competitors need ~60% longer cavities for the same linewidth. The CPO market is starved enough that a half-dead LiDAR company could still sell this at 5-20% gross margin vs. the majors' 50-65%.
items.angle: Sell-side is pitching Aeva-style SOA supply as a fix for the CPO laser shortage; the underlying engineering says it's viable only as a low-margin stopgap, not a threat to Broadcom/Lumentum's structural moat.
items.tweetA: Sell-side is pitching Aeva's leftover LiDAR SOA chips as the CPO shortage fix. The engineering reality: an 8-wavelength module needs 56+ active alignments and 8-16 isolators to make it work. Not a laser upgrade — an assembly nightmare the starved CPO market might tolerate anyway. $AEVA $AVGO $LITE
items.tweetB: Broadcom and Lumentum's real moat isn't the laser, it's cavity length — competitors need 60% longer cavities for the same linewidth, a straight mode-hop reliability tax. No SOA trick fixes that. It just gives desperate LiDAR shops a side door into 5-20% margin CPO supply while the majors keep 50-65%.

charts.desc: No charts in email — technical diagrams in the linked post.

items.title: S&P <200bps from ATH and the Fear-Greed Index is sitting in 'fear' — that's not how tops look
items.idea: Nasdaq fell ~400bps on the week vs small caps -60bps as the momentum/AI unwind continued, yet breadth stayed neutral (52% of stocks above the 20D, 56% above the 50D) and the Advance-Decline line sits near highs. Despite the S&P trading within 200bps of all-time highs, the Fear-Greed Index has reverted to 'fear' territory.
items.angle: Fear near all-time highs is the classic signature of a rotation, not a top — the damage is concentrated in AI/momentum names while the broader tape holds up.
items.tweetA: S&P is <200bps from ATH and the Fear-Greed index is sitting in 'fear.' That combination almost never shows up at real market tops — tops come with everyone euphoric, not scared. This reads like sector rotation wearing an index costume.
items.tweetB: Nasdaq -400bps on the week, small caps -60bps. Same week, same tape. If you're only watching the index you're missing that the AI/momentum unwind is doing surgical damage while breadth (52% above the 20D) stays neutral. The pain is concentrated, not systemic — yet.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — Fear-Greed Index overlaid against the S&P going back years, directly illustrating the 'fear near highs' divergence. Also includes STD-channel charts on SPY/QQQ/IWM/DJIA.

items.title: BTC defended $58-59k three times in three weeks while ETH quietly took share from SOL
items.idea: BTC repeatedly defended the $58-59k level through late June/early July (last test July 1st) before rallying to ~$65k, while SOLBTC pumped +22% then bled it all back against BTC as capital rotated into ETH — ETHBTC ran from 0.027 to ~0.03 (+10%) over two weeks even as most alts underperformed.
items.angle: This ETH/SOL rotation is happening in the same window the memory-stock trade (tradfi's AI proxy) violently unwound — worth checking whether crypto beta and AI-momentum beta are starting to trade as one macro factor.
items.tweetA: SOL pumped +22% vs BTC in late June, then gave it all back — meanwhile ETHBTC quietly ground from 0.027 to 0.03. Nobody's talking about the ETH/SOL rotation because everyone's staring at Micron's chart. Same 'unwind the 2026 momentum darlings' trade, two different tapes.
items.tweetB: BTC has defended $58-59k three separate times since late June. Three tests of the same level in three weeks is either distribution or the strongest support base of the cycle — the fact alts kept bleeding while BTC held tells you which one the market is actually pricing.

items.title: Sandisk was +900% YTD at its top — the same week Micron's earnings beat marked the memory trade's peak
items.idea: Micron and Sandisk were up +330% and +900% YTD respectively at their 2026 highs; Micron's late-June earnings beat pushed it to an ATH of $1,250 — which also marked the top of the trade. A violent downtrend followed into July as capital rotated back into hyperscalers on fears that AI capex (and therefore memory demand) had been over-estimated.
items.angle: A +900% YTD stock topping on its sister company's 'good' earnings beat is textbook exhaustion — the capex-overestimation fear is the market's first real stress test of the 2026 AI infrastructure supercycle thesis.
items.tweetA: Sandisk was up +900% YTD at its 2026 top. Nine hundred percent. It topped the same week Micron beat earnings and hit its ATH — that's not coincidence, that's 'sell the last good news' exhaustion. When your best print becomes your top, the trade was already over before anyone said so.
items.tweetB: The memory unwind is really a referendum on one number: did hyperscalers over-order for AI capex? If yes, this is the first crack in the 2026 AI infrastructure supercycle thesis — not a China model, not a rate move, a straightforward 'we bought too many chips' correction.

items.title: Robinhood Chain's launch turned into an instant liquidity subsidy for its ecosystem — and a warning shot for dYdX
items.idea: Robinhood Chain (an Arbitrum L2) launched July 1st: memecoin CASHCAT ran $8M→$220M mcap, LIT (its official perp DEX) ran $1.8→$2.7, ARB +30% and UNI +35%. DYDX gave back a prior 2x move after Robinhood picked Lighter over dYdX for its new Arcus stock-trading platform, despite dYdX's own competing announcement.
items.angle: A brokerage with tens of millions of retail accounts just handed a small L2 ecosystem an instant attention/liquidity subsidy — the DYDX reversal is the cleanest example yet of platform-picks-winner risk in the L2/perp-DEX land grab.
items.tweetA: Robinhood didn't launch a token, it launched a liquidity injection. CASHCAT: $8M to $220M mcap. ARB +30%. UNI +35%. All from one brokerage choosing a chain. This is what platform risk looks like in DeFi now — not smart contract risk, distribution risk.
items.tweetB: DYDX 2x'd on rumors of a Robinhood tie-up, then gave it all back the moment Robinhood picked Lighter instead. If your biggest catalyst is 'maybe a fintech partners with us,' you don't have a moat, you have a lottery ticket.

items.title: Venice raised $70M at a $1B valuation — and the token dropped 30% anyway
items.idea: $VVV (Venice) announced a $70M raise at a $1B valuation on July 1st, popping +22% initially — but the raise was for the Venice equity, not the VVV token, triggering FUD and a -30% drawdown. A follow-up announcement that 5% of API credit purchases would buy back and burn VVV clawed back +18% off the bottom.
items.angle: A live case study in the dual-token/equity structure problem crypto keeps re-learning: a headline raise tells you nothing about token-holder claims unless the cash-flow bridge is explicit — and the market now prices that gap in real time instead of waiting for it to surface.
items.tweetA: Venice raised $70M at a $1B valuation and the TOKEN dropped 30%. Read that again. When equity and token are separate claims on the same company, 'we raised money' is not automatically bullish for you — it's bullish for whoever owns the equity.
items.tweetB: It took one buyback-and-burn announcement (5% of API credits) to claw back +18% after the raise tanked VVV -30%. The lesson for every dual-token project launching in 2026: explicit value-accrual mechanics aren't optional anymore, the market prices their absence immediately.

items.title: ZEC is up +25% on a bug fix, not a feature — while the rest of alts bleed
items.idea: The Zcash team confirmed in early July that the 'Ironwood' upgrade (activating late July) resolves the long-standing potential 'infinite mint' exploit concern. ZEC is up +25% since the announcement and has been one of the most consistently strong assets in the market through the broader alt bleed.
items.angle: A protocol removing a tail-risk overhang is being rewarded more than most utility launches this cycle — a signal that capital is currently pricing de-risking over growth.
items.tweetA: ZEC +25% since Zcash confirmed the infinite-mint exploit gets patched in the Ironwood upgrade. Notice what's NOT happening here — no new feature, no partnership, just removing a scary tail risk. In a market this jumpy, 'we fixed the thing that could kill us' is apparently the best catalyst you can have.
items.tweetB: Everything else in alts is bleeding. ZEC just posted one of the market's most consistent uptrends on a bug fix. That's a tell about current risk appetite: capital is rewarding de-risking over growth right now, not the other way around.

items.title: HYPE couldn't hold a legitimately bullish catalyst — that's the real topping signal
items.idea: Hyperliquid's HYPE has shown declining daily volume since its early-June top, fading ETF volumes since the May launch, and a lower high at $73 on July 7. A July 15 CXMT pre-IPO listing briefly pumped it $63→$69 before the move got rejected and price dumped to $58 — even as HIP-3 volume (driven by memory-sector volatility) should theoretically be bullish for HYPE.
items.angle: When a genuinely bullish catalyst can't break a lower-high structure, that's a stronger bearish signal than price action alone — positioning, not news flow, is in control.
items.tweetA: HYPE got a legitimately bullish catalyst — HIP-3 volume surging off memory-stock volatility — and it still couldn't hold $69, dumping to $58. When good news can't break a lower-high structure, that's not noise, that's distribution.
items.tweetB: Declining ETF volume since May, declining daily volume since June, lower high in July. Nobody needed the CXMT pre-IPO pump-and-dump to tell you HYPE was topping — it was already telling you with volume for six weeks before price finally agreed.

items.title: The Copper-to-Nasdaq ratio is on the verge of a major breakout
items.idea: Tavi Costa argues the Copper-to-Nasdaq ratio is nearing a major breakout and frames a 5-7 year capital-flows thesis around owning 'the irreplaceable materials powering AI' rather than 'tech companies priced for perfection.'
items.angle: A breaking copper/Nasdaq ratio is a classic late-cycle rotation signal — inputs starting to outperform the thing they feed — echoing the same rotation-not-top read in the Eliant breadth data above.
items.tweetA: Copper-to-Nasdaq ratio on the verge of breaking out, per Tavi Costa. If that holds, it's the same signal gold-vs-S&P gave in 2000 and 2008: the inputs are about to outperform the thing they feed. The AI trade isn't dying, it's about to change which ticker it lives under.
items.tweetB: 'Own the irreplaceable materials powering AI, not the software priced for perfection' — that's not a hedge, that's a bet that data-center buildout constraints (copper, power, cooling) become the scarcity story before compute does.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (email was a teaser for the full report).

items.title: NYC cash assistance hits 864,999 recipients — largest caseload in three decades, $2.7B and rising
items.idea: 864,999 New Yorkers received cash assistance in 2025 (~$2.6-2.7B in payments, the largest caseload in roughly three decades per NY Post), alongside a 12-block homeless encampment now running from W34th to W46th St near the Intrepid Museum. The piece frames cash assistance, shelter capacity, voucher bottlenecks (landlords refusing vouchers even where legally required — see the Greystar civil-rights case) and encampment throughput as one interlocked system buying time to keep marginal households out of the costlier emergency-shelter system.
items.angle: A $2.7B single-city safety-net line item, still growing, is a fiscal-cliff indicator hiding inside a 'homelessness' headline — the next checkpoint is HRA's August data posting, which sets up a fall NYC budget fight regardless of the political framing.
items.tweetA: 864,999 New Yorkers on cash assistance, $2.7B in payments — the largest caseload in three decades, in ONE city. That's not a homelessness story, that's a line item about to force a fall NYC budget fight regardless of who's mayor.
items.tweetB: The real bottleneck isn't money, it's landlords refusing vouchers even where required — see the Greystar civil rights case. You can fund unlimited housing subsidies and it doesn't matter if the private market won't take the paper. That's a supply-side problem no cash-assistance check fixes.

items.title: This week's featured chart: Africa's mineral advantage
items.idea: McKinsey's Week in Charts leads with a chart on Africa's mineral reserve advantage — no body text beyond the title in this digest email, just the chart image and a link to the full page.
items.angle: Worth opening directly — Africa critical-minerals framing from McKinsey typically quantifies reserve share of cobalt/lithium/rare earths, directly relevant to the EV/battery and China-supply-chain-dependence trade.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's featured chart this week: Africa's mineral advantage. No text, just the chart — but the underlying story (Africa holding the reserve base for the metals everyone needs to de-risk from China) is the one critical-minerals chart worth screenshotting this week.
items.tweetB: Every 'critical minerals' panic is really a 'who controls the African reserve base' question wearing a different country's flag. Worth pulling this chart before the next DRC/cobalt headline turns it into a trade instead of a slide.

charts.desc: Chart image not extractable from plaintext — open the link to view the reserve-share breakdown directly.

items.title: McKinsey packages 'navigating geopolitical uncertainty' as this week's own headline chart
items.idea: A second featured chart in the same digest: 'Navigating geopolitical uncertainty' — again image-only in this email, title and link only.
items.angle: When McKinsey turns geopolitical uncertainty into a recurring headline chart instead of a one-off report, that's a signal the boardroom conversation has permanently shifted from special-topic to baseline planning.
items.tweetA: McKinsey put out a chart this week literally titled 'Navigating geopolitical uncertainty.' Even the most buttoned-up consulting shop in the world is telling clients the base case is now uncertainty, not a scenario — that's a tell about what's showing up in board decks industry-wide.
items.tweetB: When McKinsey packages 'geopolitical uncertainty' as a recurring headline chart instead of a special report, that's a signal the C-suite conversation has permanently shifted — this isn't a special topic anymore, it's baseline planning.

charts.desc: Chart image not extractable from plaintext — open the link to view directly.

items.title: 'From likes to buys' — McKinsey charts AI collapsing the social-to-purchase funnel
items.idea: Third chart in the digest: 'From likes to buys,' on AI tools shortening the path from social browsing to purchase. Image-only in the email body; title and link only.
items.angle: The AI angle on social commerce isn't better product discovery, it's AI agents running the discovery-to-purchase loop autonomously — a wedge for shopping agents that transfers margin from ad platforms to whoever owns the checkout API.
items.tweetA: 'From likes to buys' — McKinsey's chart on AI collapsing the gap between seeing a product on social and buying it. Every basis point of friction removed from that funnel is a basis point of margin transferred from ad platforms to whoever owns the checkout API.
items.tweetB: Social commerce has been 'the next big thing' for a decade. The AI angle is different: it's not better product discovery, it's AI agents running the discovery-to-purchase loop autonomously. That's a wedge for shopping agents, not just a UX tweak.

charts.desc: Chart image not extractable from plaintext — open the link to view directly.
